messages.content:
### Lesson 4: Statistics with JASP – Summary

This lesson provides an introduction to using JASP for statistical analysis, specifically tailored for psychology and social-science students.

#### **1. Why JASP?**
*   **Intuitive Interface:** Unlike SPSS, JASP features a straightforward menu structure [S.7].
*   **Real-time Output:** Analysis results appear automatically on the right-hand side of the screen as you interact with settings; changes are updated instantly [S.7]. 
*   **Integrated R:** JASP is built on an R platform, allowing users to toggle R code visibility. This provides a "springboard" for those interested in coding while remaining accessible for those who prefer a point-and-click experience [S.7].

#### **2. Initial Setup**
*   **Installation:** JASP is free and available at [jasp-stats.org](https://jasp-stats.org) [S.11].
*   **Customization:** Beginners are encouraged to adjust interface settings, such as using APA-approved sans-serif fonts (e.g., Calibri, Arial), adjusting zoom levels, and setting decimal precision for results to 3 decimal places [S.13, S.14].

#### **3. Data Management**
*   **File Types:** JASP can create its own files or import data from Excel via the **Comma-Separated Values (.csv)** format [S.17, S.24].
*   **Variable Types:** Users must define variables appropriately to enable correct statistical tests [S.18]:
    *   **Scale:** For continuous variables (e.g., age).
    *   **Nominal:** For categorical variables (e.g., gender).
    *   **Ordinal:** For ranked data (e.g., Likert scales).
*   **Advanced Features:**
    *   **Labelling:** Users can assign meaningful labels to numerical data (e.g., 1 = Male) [S.19].
    *   **Filtering:** Data can be subsetted by categorical groups or specific criteria (e.g., excluding outliers) using the "Advanced Filtering" funnel icon [S.27, S.29, S.31].
    *   **Data Syncing:** JASP can synchronize with .csv files, allowing users to perform complex data manipulation in Excel that immediately updates in the JASP interface [S.23].
    *   **Computing Variables:** New variables can be created via drag-and-drop formulas (e.g., summing item scores) or R code (e.g., `ifElse` commands for categorical transforms) [S.32, S.41].

#### **4. Analysis Workflow**
*   **Modes:** Users toggle between **Data View Mode** (for editing and preparing data) and **Analysis Mode** (where the "Descriptives" and inferential statistical tests are located) [S.39, S.45].

Lesson 4: Statistics with JASP - Presentation5 slides
Statistics with JASP: First Steps
  • Point-and-click statistical analysis for psychology students.
  • Accessible introduction to JASP for beginners (p. 7).

### Summary: Statistics with JASP: First Steps for Psychology Students

This guide, authored by Rebecca Denniss and Stanislava Naneva, serves as an introductory manual for using JASP for statistical analysis, specifically tailored for psychology undergraduates (p. 3, 7).

**Key Objectives and Content**
The publication provides practical "how-to" instructions for students with no prior experience in statistics packages. It does not provide data sets or output interpretations, as those components are intended for classroom instruction (p. 7). The book covers:
*   **Getting Started:** Installation, accessibility settings, and data file management (including importing from Excel) (p. 5).
*   **Data Handling:** Sorting, filtering, and computing new variables (p. 5).
*   **Descriptive Statistics:** Utilizing plots, graphs, and tables (p. 5).
*   **Inferential Statistics:** Conducting t-tests, correlations, and chi-squared tests (p. 6).

**Why JASP?**
The authors promote JASP as a superior alternative to software like SPSS for beginners due to its intuitive design:
*   **Interface:** Unlike the complex menu structure of SPSS, JASP features a straightforward interface where outputs generate automatically on the right side of the screen (p. 7).
*   **Dynamic Interaction:** Users can adjust parameters and see immediate updates, preventing the confusion caused by building up large quantities of excess output (p. 7).
*   **Pedagogical Focus:** By simplifying the "how" of performing analyses, the software allows students to focus on the "why" of statistical reasoning (p. 7).

**Technical Foundation**
JASP acts as a graphical user interface (GUI) built on the R platform (p. 7). While it hides the complexity of coding for those who simply need to perform analyses, it remains transparent enough that users interested in programming can view the underlying R code (p. 7).

**Licensing**
The work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License (p. 4, 8).

This summary covers the essential procedures for setting up and managing data in JASP, based on pages [S.16] through [S.20].

**Opening Existing Data**
To open a saved JASP file, use the collapsed menu icon to navigate through Open > Computer > Browse and select your file [S.16].

**Creating New Data Files**
When starting a new file, the data entry structure follows a standard format: rows represent individual participants, while columns represent variables [S.17].

**Defining Variables**
To configure a variable, double-click the column header to access settings [S.18]:
1. Name and Metadata: Provide a short name, a longer descriptive name, and a description to help organize large datasets [S.18].
2. Column Types: Assign the correct type to ensure appropriate analysis options are available:
- Scale: Used for continuous variables, such as age [S.18].
- Nominal: Used for categorical variables, such as gender [S.18].
- Ordinal: Used for ordered scales, such as Likert-type items [S.19].

**Managing Categorical Data**
For nominal variables, you must map numerical values to specific labels using the Label Editor [S.19, S.20]:
1. Assign numerical values (e.g., 1, 2, 3) to the categorical groups within the data column [S.19].
2. Use the Label Editor to replace those numbers with meaningful names (e.g., Male, Female, Non-binary), which will then be displayed in the data column [S.20].

This guide covers essential data management and manipulation techniques for JASP, as outlined in pages 21–36.

**Data Handling and Synchronization**
JASP allows users to manage and organize data through advanced features. Since direct sorting of data columns is not natively supported in version 0.18.3.0, users can link JASP to a .csv file using the Synchronisation icon. By selecting "Open datafile with default spreadsheet editor," changes made in Excel (such as sorting data) will be reflected in JASP once the file is saved (p. 23, 26).

**Filtering Data**
Filtering allows researchers to focus on specific subsets of participants or exclude cases without deleting data. There are two primary methods:
1. Categorical Filtering: In the Variable View, users can apply a filter by clicking the checkmark under the Filter column for specific groups, changing it to an $x$ mark to exclude those participants from subsequent analyses (p. 27).
2. Advanced Filtering: For complex criteria, the funnel icon opens an advanced menu. This allows for multi-variable filtering using logical operators. For example, to filter by condition and score, one can use the $\neq$ operator for categorical groups and comparative operators like $\ge$ for numerical, continuous variables (p. 29-30).

**Data Integrity and Exclusions**
Advanced filtering is also an effective way to handle outlier management. By setting specific inclusion or exclusion criteria, researchers can effectively remove individual cases (rows) from current statistical tests without permanently erasing the raw data from the file (p. 30).

This section outlines how to perform data exploration, descriptive statistical analysis, and visualization in JASP.

### Data Preparation and Manipulation
- Advanced Filtering: Allows you to exclude specific participants from analyses without deleting their data, using criteria such as $\neq$ [number] [S.37].
- Computing Variables: You can create new variables using calculations or logic.
  - Computed with drag-and-drop: Ideal for summing variables (e.g., creating a "Total" score) using mathematical operators [S.39, S.40].
  - Computed with R code: Useful for recoding variables, such as transforming continuous data into categorical groups (e.g., $IfElse(Age \leq 20, \text{"young"}, \text{"old"})$) [S.41].
- Outlier Detection: You can generate Z-scores by using the $zScores()$ function to identify values typically beyond $\pm 3$ standard deviations [S.50].

### Descriptive Statistics
- Analysis Mode: Accessed via the "Analyses" button, this mode allows for generating descriptive statistics and plots [S.45].
- Statistic Computation: You can calculate descriptive statistics for entire datasets or split by categorical groups. Common statistics include mean and standard deviation, which update automatically as you refine your selections [S.46, S.47].

### Data Visualization
- Basic Plots: Includes tools to visualize distributions (histograms with density lines), correlations (scatterplots), and interval plots (error bars) [S.52, S.53].
- Customizable Plots: Offers advanced options like Boxplots (with outlier labels) and Scatterplots (with regression lines), allowing for additions like violin and jitter elements for enhanced interpretation [S.55, S.56, S.57].
- Frequency Tables: Used to observe the distribution of categorical variables and determine sample sizes ($N$) [S.58].

### Exporting and Editing
- Plots generated in JASP can be resized directly within the interface by dragging the corners [S.59].
- Images can be exported in formats like .png using the "Save Image As" function found in the output menu for each graph [S.60].

This section (Chapter IV) covers the application of inferential statistics within JASP. The following are the key analytical procedures described:

**Independent t-test and Mann-Whitney U**
These tests are used to compare the means or distributions of two independent groups. The independent t-test is applied when data are normally distributed, while the Mann-Whitney U test serves as the non-parametric alternative for non-normal data (p. 63).

**Paired t-test and Wilcoxon Signed Ranks**
These tests are designed for dependent samples where the same group is measured twice (e.g., pre-test and post-test). Similar to the previous tests, the paired t-test is for parametric data, and the Wilcoxon Signed Ranks test is the non-parametric equivalent (p. 68).

**Pearson and Spearman Correlations**
These methods assess the strength and direction of a relationship between two continuous variables. The Pearson correlation measures linear relationships, whereas the Spearman correlation measures monotonic relationships using ranked data (p. 72).

**Chi-squared Test**
This test is used to determine if there is a significant association between two categorical variables by comparing observed frequencies to expected frequencies (p. 76).

Based on the provided text, the document covers foundational steps for using JASP for statistical analysis. Since the provided document ends on page 81 (the "About the Authors" section), there are no pages 82–87 available to summarize. The relevant content from the initial pages is summarized below:

### Overview of JASP
JASP is an open-source, user-friendly statistical software built on the R platform. It is designed to be more intuitive than SPSS, featuring a straightforward menu structure and real-time output synchronization. Students can observe the underlying R code for analyses if desired, or simply focus on the statistical interpretation.

### Getting Started and Accessibility
* Installation: JASP is available for free at jasp-stats.org and can be installed on Windows, Mac, or Chromebooks.
* Interface Customization: Users can adjust settings under the "Preferences" menu. Recommendations include using APA-approved sans-serif fonts (e.g., Arial), selecting a theme (Light or Dark), and adjusting zoom levels for visual comfort.
* Result Settings: It is recommended to fix decimal places to 3 (to accommodate standard $p$-value reporting) and enable R syntax if users wish to learn about the underlying operations.

### Data Management
* File Handling: Users can import data from Excel or create new datasets directly within JASP.
* Structure: Data organization follows a standard format where each row represents a unique participant and each column represents a specific variable.
* Flexibility: The software allows for sorting, filtering (by categorical variables), and computing new variables, providing a robust environment for data cleaning and preparation before performing inferential tests.

### Pedagogical Scope
The guide focuses on "how-to" instructions for students, covering:
* Descriptive statistics, plots, and tables.
* Inferential analyses, including independent and paired t-tests, Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon Signed Ranks, Pearson and Spearman correlations, and Chi-squared tests.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why does the author suggest JASP is, for many students, an improvement over SPSS for learning statistics?
options: It provides a more intuitive menu structure and allows users to see how changes to settings impact outputs immediately.
It automatically generates comprehensive written interpretations for all statistical tests conducted.
It requires users to write their own R code, which is better for long-term skill development.
It is the only statistics software capable of performing complex psychometric analyses.
questions.explanation: The text notes that SPSS can be confusing due to a complex, non-intuitive menu structure and a lack of clear links to output. JASP addresses this with a straightforward structure and automatic, real-time output updates, allowing students to focus on the 'why' of the analysis.

questions.question: What is the primary technical limitation mentioned regarding the management of data columns in JASP version 0.18.3.0?
options: It is unable to import files that were originally created in Microsoft Excel.
It does not have a built-in feature to sort columns of data in ascending or descending order.
It cannot handle more than two categorical variables at a time.
It requires all data to be manually entered because it cannot accept external CSV files.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states in Chapter II, Section 5, that sorting columns of data in ascending or descending order is not a feature available in JASP version 0.18.3.0.

questions.question: When setting up a new variable in JASP, how does the 'Column type' setting affect the user's experience?
options: It forces the user to write R code if they select 'Scale' instead of 'Nominal'.
It determines which statistical analyses JASP will allow the user to perform with that variable.
It converts nominal numbers into descriptive text strings automatically during data entry.
It automatically formats the visual appearance of the graphs generated for that variable.
questions.explanation: The author explains that selecting the correct column type (Scale, Nominal, or Ordinal) is essential because JASP restricts certain analyses based on the variable type.

questions.question: If you want to ensure your data changes made in an external spreadsheet are reflected in JASP, what must you do after editing the CSV file?
options: You must manually delete the dataset in JASP and re-import it.
You must convert the CSV file back into an Excel (.xlsx) format.
You must restart the JASP application for the handshake to initiate.
You must save the CSV file in order for the changes to sync with the JASP file.
questions.explanation: According to the instruction on 'Sorting Data & Linking Data to Excel Spreadsheets', once you save the .csv file in your spreadsheet editor, the corresponding column in JASP will update automatically.

questions.question: Regarding the use of 'string' variables (words) instead of numerical values for categorical data in JASP, why does the author offer a word of caution?
options: JASP cannot produce labels for string variables, making the output unreadable.
If the words are not entered with exact consistency, JASP may interpret them as different categories.
JASP will not accept string variables under any circumstances.
JASP requires that string variables be converted to binary code before analysis.
questions.explanation: The text warns that while JASP allows string variables, users must be consistent because any slight variation in spelling will lead JASP to think there are additional categories.

cards.front: What are the two major advantages of JASP compared to SPSS for students?
cards.back: JASP has a more intuitive, straightforward menu structure and outputs appear automatically on the right-hand side of the screen as you update analyses.

cards.front: What underlying statistical program provides the foundation for JASP?
What are the three JASP column types defined in the book?
cards.back: Scale (continuous variables), Nominal (categorical variables), and Ordinal (variables that use a rank/scale).

cards.front: When creating a new variable, what is the 'Label editor' used for?
cards.back: Assigning meaningful names to categorical variable values (e.g., mapping '1' to 'Male' and '2' to 'Female').

cards.front: In what specific file format must an Excel spreadsheet be saved to be imported into JASP?
What recommended interface settings improve readability for JASP?
cards.back: Use APA-approved sans serif fonts (like Arial or Calibri), a zoom level of approximately 130%, and choosing a theme (Light or Dark) that causes the least eye strain.

cards.front: How do you handle data sorting in JASP given that there is no native sort button in version 0.18.3.0?
cards.back: Use the Synchronisation feature to link the JASP file to a .csv file, sort the data in an external spreadsheet editor (like Excel), and save the file to update JASP.

cards.front: How do you filter data to analyze specific groups or conditions?
cards.back: Open 'Variable View' for the categorical variable and click the tick mark under the 'Filter' column for the groups you wish to exclude.

cards.front: In JASP, why is it recommended to fix the number of decimal places for tables to 3?
cards.back: Because while most values are rounded to 2 decimal places, p-values specifically often require reporting to 3 decimal places.

cards.front: What happens in JASP when you change a setting or perform a new analysis while the program is open?
cards.back: The output updates automatically on the right-hand side, allowing users to see the direct connection between their actions and the results.

slides.title: Statistics with JASP: First Steps
bullets: Point-and-click statistical analysis for psychology students.
Accessible introduction to JASP for beginners (p. 7).
slides.notes: This guide provides a straightforward, user-friendly entry point into statistics, focusing on the 'why' rather than the 'how' of data analysis.

slides.title: Chapter I: Getting Started
bullets: Installing JASP from Software Centre or jasp-stats.org (p. 5).
Customizing interface, fonts, and result decimals (p. 7-8).
Opening existing files vs. creating new datasets (p. 10-11).
slides.notes: This chapter covers setting up your workspace and ensuring data is correctly loaded, which is the foundational step for any statistical analysis.

slides.title: Chapter II: Data Handling
bullets: Syncing JASP with .csv files to use Excel tools (p. 23).
Using data filters to focus on specific categories (p. 27).
Advanced filtering with custom rules for exclusions (p. 31).
Computing new variables via drag-and-drop or R code (p. 32-35).
slides.notes: Data manipulation is key to preparing your dataset. We explore how to manage variables and filter participants to ensure accurate results.

slides.title: Chapter III: Descriptive Statistics
bullets: Generating key descriptive statistics for sample summaries (p. 39).
Creating and customizing basic data plots (p. 46-49).
Managing tables and exporting high-quality visuals (p. 52-53).
slides.notes: Visualizing and summarizing your data allows you to understand distributions and identify patterns before performing formal inferential testing.

slides.title: Chapter IV: Inferential Statistics
bullets: Applying Mann-Whitney U and Wilcoxon Signed Ranks (p. 63, 68).
Executing Chi-squared tests for categorical data (p. 76).
slides.notes: Finally, we apply statistical tests to your data, moving from simple descriptions to drawing inferences about your research questions.
